coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent probe based on carbon dots and Zn-doped CdTe QDs for detection of 6-Mercaptopurine
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Tian
Gan, Zhiwen
Zhen, Shujun
Hu, Yongmei
Hu, Xiaoli
coredata.dc:description: Due to the well-known side effects on human health caused by 6-Mercaptopurine (6-MP), it is necessary to develop a simple to operate method for determination of 6-MP. In this work, a simple and easy ratiometric fluorescence probe was fabricated based on two kind of quantum dots with different emission. The probe was composed of blue emission carbon dots (CDs) and yellow emission Zn-doped CdTe quantum dots (ZnCdTe QDs) capped with GSH. The fluorescence intensity of ZnCdTe QDs could be selectively quenched by 6-MP based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). At the same time, the fluorescence intensity of CDs basically stayed constant, and the fluorescence intensity ratio (F 445/F 565) showed a linear response to the concentration of 6-MP. The designed probe of ZnCdTe QDs/CDs displayed a wide and comparable detection range from 1.0 to 100.0 μmol L−1, and the detection limit reached 0.46 μmol L−1. Furthermore, the method has been realized for monitoring the concentration of 6-MP in human serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence probe
CDs
ZnCdTe QDs
6-Mercaptopurine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Zn–CdTe QDs and CdTe QDs
Detection of 6-MP in human serum samples
Feasibility and sensing mechanism of 6-MP detection
Selectivity of ZnCdTe QDs/CDs system for 6-MP detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM image of CDs. (B) The HRTEM image of CDs. (C) The XRD spectrum of CDs. (D) The FTIR spectrum of CDs.

(A) The TEM image of ZnCdTe QDs. (B) The HRTEM image of ZnCdTe QDs. (C) The XRD spectrum of ZnCdTe QDs. (D)The XPS full scan spectrum of ZnCdTe QDs and CdTe QDs. (E)The XPS spectrum of Zn 2p. (F)The FTIR spectrum of ZnCdTe QDs.
(A) The different pH of Δ(F 565/F 445) of fluorometric ratio. (B) The reaction time. (C) The volume of ZnCdTe QDs (1.0 mg mL−1).

(A) Ratiometric fluorescence spectra of different concentrations of 6-MP from 1.0 to 100.0 μmol L−1. (B) Calibration curve of F 445/F 565 for 6-MP detection with the concentration from 1.0 to 100.0 μmol L−1.

(A) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs. (B) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of ZnCdTe QDs. (C) Fluorescence spectra of CDs, CDs+6-MP, 6-MP, ZnCdTe QDs, ZnCdTe QDs + CDs, ZnCdTe QDs + CDs+6-MP. (D) UV–vis spectra of 6-MP, ZnCdTe QDs, ZnCdTe+6-MP. (E) UV–vis spectra of 6-MP and Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of ZnCdTe QDs. (F) Fluorescence decay curves of ZnCdTe QDs and ZnCdTe+6-MP. (G) The selectivity of 6-MP (Ca2+, K+, Na+, arginine, glutamate, serine, sarcosine, leucine and threonine were 1000.0 μmol L−1, and 6-MP, 6-TG, glucose, guanine, adenine, GSH and Cys were 100.0 μmol L−1).
Schematic illustration of ratiometric fluorescence sensing for 6-MP.
The determination results (N = 3) of 6-MP in human serum.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent probe based on carbon dots and Zn-doped CdTe QDs for detection of 6-Mercaptopurine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chengdu Second People's Hospital, Chengdu, 610017, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chengdu Second People’s Hospital, Chengdu 610017, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the well-known side effects on human health caused by 6-Mercaptopurine (6-MP), it is necessary to develop a simple to operate method for determination of 6-MP. In this work, a simple and easy ratiometric fluorescence probe was fabricated based on two kind of quantum dots with different emission. The probe was composed of blue emission carbon dots (CDs) and yellow emission Zn-doped CdTe quantum dots (ZnCdTe QDs) capped with GSH. The fluorescence intensity of ZnCdTe QDs could be selectively quenched by 6-MP based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). At the same time, the fluorescence intensity of CDs basically stayed constant, and the fluorescence intensity ratio (F 445/F 565) showed a linear response to the concentration of 6-MP. The designed probe of ZnCdTe QDs/CDs displayed a wide and comparable detection range from 1.0 to 100.0 μmol L−1, and the detection limit reached 0.46 μmol L−1. Furthermore, the method has been realized for monitoring the concentration of 6-MP in human serum samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: 6- Mercaptopurine (6-MP), as an analogue of thiopurine, could inhibit tumor cell growth in that it mainly interferes the purine synthesis process important for DNA and RNA biosynthesis [1,2]. In view of the properties, 6-MP have long been used as the effective clinical agents of antimetabolite and antineoplastic. It even could be in the therapy of many diseases such as acute myelocytic leukemia, acute lymphoblastic leukemia, inflammatory bowel diseases, rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus and chorionic epithelial cell carcinoma etc [3–6]. However, 6-MP serves as a cytotoxic anti-tumor drug, which may lead to myelosuppression, digestive disorder, hyperuricemia, renal and hepatic problems in the inappropriate dosage [7]. Therefore, it has an important biological significance to monitor the concentration of 6-MP in human body. So far, different analytical method has been established to detect 6-MP, mainly including resonance Rayleigh light scattering [8], electrochemical methods [9], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10], chemiluminescence [11], Raman spectroscopy [12] and colorimetry et [13]. Compared to these methods with limited applications, fluorescent spectroscopy has distinct advantage such as rapid response, analytical stability and effective cost in biological analysis. However, most fluorescent probes were a single signal mode, the accuracy and sensitivity of probe may be interfered by concentration of fluorescent materials and instrument parameters. The ratiometric fluorescence detection system owned dual-signal to correct variables independent of the analyte, thus improving the result anti-interference and self-calibration ability. Although previous works have been reported to detect 6-MP in fluorescence sensing, the proposed method requires material modification, functionalization, or the addition of specific reagents to form specific recognition with 6-MP which greatly increases the cost and time for detection [14]. Therefore, it is necessary to design a simple and easy to operate ratiometric probe to detect 6-MP.

In recently years, some fluorescent nanomaterials including organic small molecules, and noble metallic nanoparticles, organic fluorescent dye, fluorescent metal-organic framework and Quantum dots (QDs) were highly developed. QDs are kinds of colloidal semiconductor nanocrystals with fluorescence properties, showing outstanding advantages such as excellent photostability, tunable emission, antiphotobleaching ability [15], which are conducive to a wide range of applications in electronic materials science, diagnosis [16], optoelectronic fields [17,18], bioimaging and fluorescent sensing [19]. Common fluorescent dyes have a non-negligible toxic effect, and the synthesis of some fluorescent MOF materials is complicated, while carbon dots (CDs) are also a kind of fluorescent materials with excellent structure, featuring high biocompatibility and low toxicity. At present, QDs and CDs were popularly utilized in fluorescent sensing. For example, You et al. developed a ratiometric fluorescent test pen composed of carbon dots and CdTe quantum dots for portable assay of silver ion on paper [20]. Zhao et al. designed a multifunctional fluorescent platform for Glucose and pH detection based on the destruction of passivation or doping groups of the material [21]. Chen and his co-work constructed dual mode ratiometric probe based on carbon dots and CuInS2/ZnS quantum dots for detection of chlorotetracycline [22]. Based on the above, it is feasible to construct a fluorescence probe based on CDs and QDs for 6-MP detection. According to previous studies, the doping of transition element ions in CdTe could improve the fluorescence performance of quantum dots [23]. In this work, Zn-doped CdTe QDs (ZnCdTe QDs) was synthesized through a one-step aqueous route without toxic solvent and complex synthesis steps. In addition, Zn-doped CdTe QDs can reduce the proportion of toxic cadmium content, which could reduce the toxicity of the synthesized quantum dots, thus increasing biocompatibility and facilitating the safety detection of biological system [24]. It is of great significance to combine ZnCdTe QDs with CDs for sensitive detection of 6-MP.

In this work, we successfully synthesized CdTe QDs doped with zinc ions by one-step method. A method for determining 6-MP was established with highly sensitivity and rapid response by mixing the blue fluorescent CDs and yellow fluorescent ZnCdTe QDs (see Scheme 1 ). The hybrid probe had dual emission peaks at 445 nm and 565 nm under the excitation of 370 nm single wavelength. As an internal reference signal, CDs showed no obvious response to the addition of 6-MP and the fluorescence intensity is basically unchanged, while the fluorescence intensity of ZnCdTe QDs was selectively quenched based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) by 6-MP. Some substances with similar functional sulfhydryl groups such as glutathione (GSH) and cysteine (Cys) had no effect on the detection of the 6-MP, indicating that the probe had good selectivity. In addition, the probe was successfully used for the detection of 6-MP in human serum.

Cd(CH3COO)2·2H2O, Zn(CH3COO)2, K2TeO3, NaBH4, glutathione (GSH), CaCl2, KCl, NaCl, glucose, serine, arginine, leucine, sarcosine, glutamate, l-threonine, cysteine (Cys), guanine and Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane, HCl, NaOH, o-Phenylenediamine (OPD), 6-MP, 6-thioguanin (6-TG), Rhodamine 6G and quinine sulphate were obtained from Macklin Biochemical (Shanghai, China). Citric Acid (CA) and acetone were bought from Chongqing Beibei Chemical Reagent Company (Chongqing, China). All the reagents were analytical grade and were directly used without further treatments. The deionized water was used for preparing the corresponding solutions for later research.

Hitachi fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan, F-2500) was used for Fluorescence spectrum. TEM (The United States, Talos F200X) was utilized for morphology analysis of ZnCdTe QDs and CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, The United States, Thermo escalab 250Xi) was determined the element of the QDs by measuring the photoelectron kinetic energy channels. X-Ray Powder Diffraction (XRD) spectrum were obtained by AXS-D8 X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). The FTIR spectrum and Ultraviolet spectrum were shown by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Tokyo, Japan, FTIR-8400S) and UV-2700 spectrophotometer.

CDs was formed through a previous report [25]. The product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 8 min to remove unreacted materials and reaction byproducts. Then, the solution was dialyzing for 24 h to remove impurities by dialysis membrane (1000 MWCO). Finally, the obtained solution was kept at 4 °C for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Zn–CdTe QDs and CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: ZnCdTe QDs was synthesized according to reflux condensation producer based on previous literature with some modifications [26]. Briefly, 10.66 mg Cd(CH3COO)2·2H2O and 2.20 mg Zn(CH3COO)2 were dissolved in 10.0 mL H2O. 0.0154 g GSH was dissolved in 2.5 mL H2O and was added into the preceding solutions with magnetic stirring. Then, the pH of solution was adjusted to 11.0 by NaOH (1.0 mol L−1). When the solution was stirred with 5 min, 2.56 mg K2TeO3 to dissolve 12.5 mL H2O was put into the above mixed solution and 1.0 mL 0.53 mol L−1 NaBH4 was added in sequence. The reaction mixture was directly heated at a 105 °C oil bath pot in the oped air (the reaction time was 100 min). After a period of time, the transparent solution became orange. When the reaction stopped, the mixture was put into 50.0 ml acetone with centrifuging at high speed (10,000 rpm min−1, 8 min) to obtain the ZnCdTe QDs.
As a control, the synthesis of CdTe QDs was used the same producer without Zn(CH3COO)2 and the weight of Cd(CH3COO)2·2H2O was changed to13.32 mg.

The detection of 6-MP was carried out in Tris-HCl buffers (0.05 mol L−1, pH 8.7). The 100.0 μL of buffer solution were added to 680.0 μL of deionized water. Then, 70.0 μL of ZnCdTe QDs solutions (1.0 mg mL−1) and 100.0 μL of CDs solutions were mixed for preparing ratiometric fluorescence probe. Thereafter, various concentration of 6-MP solutions of 50.0 μL was added to above mixed system. Finally, the solution was incubated for 20.0 min at room temperature. The fluorescence intensity were recorded for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of 6-MP in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the practicability of this methods under real conditions, human serum was used as real sample. Before a certain concentration of 6-MP was spiked into serum, the serum was with some treatments to remove macromolecular protein substance [7]. The total serum concentration was diluted a hundred times and others was based on previous steps for spike-and-recovery experiment.

The size and morphology was characterized by TEM measurement to verify their as-synthesized nanostructures. The TEM images of CDs revealed their sphere-like shape and good dispersion. The particle size of CDs was 2.25 ± 0.11 nm and the lattice spacing was calculated to be 0.218 nm. The XRD pattern (Fig. 1 C) for CDs showed a broad diffraction peak at θ = 21.9o, which linked to (0 0 2) plane of graphene [27]. To further investigate the functional groups on the surface of the CDs, FTIR measurement was utilized in Fig. 1D. The broad absorption bands at around 3406.0 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of N–H/O–H. The peak at 1572.0 cm−1 was assigned to asymmetric stretching vibration of COO−1, which was related to citric acid, one of the synthetic materials of CDs [28]. The appearance peaks of 1714 cm−1, 1390.0 cm−1 and 1220.0 cm−1 were related to the stretching vibrations of C=O, C–H and C–N bonds [25,29]. The FTIR results showed that there were many hydrophilic groups on the surface of CDs, which contributed to water-soluble property of CDs.

The ZnCdTe QDs of TEM image (Fig. 2 A and B) showed spherical shape and had some agglomeration. It could be seen that the particle size of ZnCdTe QDs was about 3.85 ± 0.14 nm and the lattice spacing was about 0.356 nm after TEM image processing. The XRD (Fig. 2C) pattern showed three obvious diffraction peaks at 24.9°, 42.8° and 48.7°, respectively linking to (111), (220) and (311) plane of ZnCdTe QDs [30]. As shown in Fig. 2D, the chemical elements of Zn, Cd, Te, C and S were indicated through XPS characterization of full scan spectrum in the as-prepared ZnCdTe QDs. The element of S indicated QDs was capped by GSH. While the XPS survey of CdTe QDs only could be revealed four peaks of Cd 3d, Te 3d, C1s and S 2p, it indirectly verified the incorporation of Zn2+ into ZnCdTe QDs. Furthermore, the binding energies of specially peaks centred at 1044.9 ev and 1022.1 ev were clearly acquired, which were assigned to Zn2p1/2 and Zn2p3/2 in Fig. 2E [24]. The FTIR spectrum was clearly shown in Fig. 2F, the broad peak from 3343.0 cm−1 to 3294.0 cm−1 were assigned to N–H and O–H of stretching vibrations. The characteristic bands at 1647.4 cm−1 and 1581.3 cm−1 were ascribed to C=O and COO− bands. The stretching vibrations of C–H centred at 1402.2 cm−1 [31].

In order to make the designed probe have a more sensitive fluorescence response to 6-MP, the detection conditions were optimized. Tris buffer solution was selected to avoid possible ionic strength disturbances. To better comparing the response of the probe to 6-MP under different conditions, the abscissa is represented by the ratio of Δ(F 565/F 445). In Fig. 3 A, when the value of pH was 8.7, the probe toward determining 6-MP had the maximum response. So, the pH 8.7 was chosen to optimal condition of reaction. The reaction time was an indispensable factor to evaluating the reaction system.The reaction time was optimized from 5.0 min to 40.0 min, and the fluorescence intensity ratio remained unchanged until 20.0 min after adding 6-MP to the system in Fig. 3B. The concentration of ZnCdTe QDs was another factor, which could infect the probe performance. With the volume of ZnCdTe QDs (Fig. 3C) solutions increasing, the Δ(F 565/F 445) of ratio reached its maximum at 70.0 μL. Then, the optimized conditions were used for subsequent experiments.

For ratiometric fluorometric detection, after adding different concentrations of 6-MP, the fluorescence intensity of the two fluorescent materials was shown in Fig. 4 A. Under the optimized detection conditions, the fluorescence intensity of yellow fluorescent ZnCdTe decreased, while the fluorescence intensity of blue fluorescent CDs was basically unchanged. The corresponding change of fluorescence intensity accorded with the original intention of designing ratiometric probe. As shown in Fig. 4B, the variation of the ratiometric fluorescence signal present a good linear relationship when 6-MP was added in the concentration range of 1.0–100.0 μmol L−1. The corresponding linear regression equation was F 445/F 565 = 0.00244c 6-MP+0.39068/μM with correlation coefficient of 0.997. The limit of detection (LOD) for 6-MP was 0.46 μmol L−1. As shown in Table 1 , compared with the previously established method, the proposed method based on the dual emission had a comparable detection limit, high sensitivity and competitive detection speed. In addition, the constructed ZnCdTe QDs/CDs system of materials are easy to prepare and easy to manipulate, which showed considerable advantages.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility and sensing mechanism of 6-MP detection
ce:section.ce:para: The ZnCdTe QDs/CDs system of dual emitting ratiometric fluorescence probe was made up by mixing the solution of CDs and ZnCdTe QDs. As shown in Fig. 5 A and B, the maximum excitation and emission wavelength of the CDs was located at 370 nm and 445 nm, while the ZnCdTe QDs showed strong fluorescence peak position of 565 nm. The Quantum yield of CDs was calculated to 48.6% using the previous report [38], and the Quantum yield of ZnCdTe QDs was 11.8% [39]. In order to obtain the best fluorescence intensity of CDs, 370 nm was selected as the excitation wavelength. After mixing the two kind of fluorescent material together, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was slightly suppressed by higher concentrations of ZnCdTe QDs. Because the ZnCdTe QDs could absorb part of the excited photons, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs decreased [40]. The fluorescence intensity of the system was high, and this change did not affect the sensitivity of the probe in Fig. 5C. After the concentration of 6-MP solution was added into the system, the fluorescence intensity of characteristic peak of ZnCdTe QDs was quenched, and the fluorescence intensity of CDs was basically unchanged, which constituted the ratiometric probe for detecting 6-MP.

In order to understand the quenching mechanism between 6-MP with ZnCdTe QDs, the UV spectrophotometer was used to explore the fluorescence quenching phenomenon. The UV–Vis spectrum was shown in Fig. 5D, no new absorption peak was generated or moved in the mixed solution of ZnCdTe QDs after the addition of 6-MP, indicating that no new compound was formed between the ZnCdTe QDs and 6-MP. The above results eliminated the possibility mechanism of static quenching effect (SQE) [41,42]. At the same time, it was found that the UV absorption peak of 6-MP (Fig. 5E) overlapped with the fluorescence excitation peak of QDs which confirmed that the quenching mechanism might be inner filter effect (IFE) or FRET [43]. When referring to fluorescence quenching mechanism of IFE, the fluorescence lifetime should be basically unchanged. Based on previous work, the fluorescence lifetimes of QDs (As shown in Fig. 5F) in the presence and absence of 6-MP were obtained as 16.0 ns and 34.6 ns, respectively. As is known to all, the sulfhydryl groups have a higher binding affinity to metal ions. Therefore, the 6-MP of sulfhydryl groups were more easily absorb on the surface of ZnCdTe QDs, so as to meet the distance between donor and recipient, which was one of the occurrence of FRET [44,45]. In conclusion, the quenching mechanism of the ZnCdTe QDs might be the FRET.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of ZnCdTe QDs/CDs system for 6-MP detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to access the selectivity of the proposed ratiometric fluorescence probe, a series of substances with similar functional groups (GSH and Cys) and possible coexisting substances in human serum were investigated for their fluorescence response to the ZnCdTe QDs/CDs system. As shown in Fig. 5G, some common amino acids (such as glucose, serine, arginine, leucine, sarcosine, GSH, l-threonine, 6-TG, Cys, guanine and ATP) and some metal ions (K+, Ca2+ and Na+) were tested. Although GSH and Cys shared the same functional sulfhydryl group as 6-MP, their effect on the fluorescence of the ratiometric probe was tolerable. Furthermore, 6-TG which could be used as an anticancer drug showed negligible effects on probes. Thus, according to the diagram above, it was confirmed that the proposed method had the ability of selective detection for most common substances.

In order to verify the practicability of this probe, the fluorescence probe under the optimized conditions was used to detect 6-MP in the human serum. The fresh human serum was obtained from healthy volunteer. However, proteins or macromolecules in serum may absorb the excitation wavelength of 370 nm, which could affect the fluorescence intensity of the detection of ZnCdTe QDs/CDs system. Therefore, the serum had to undergo some treatments before it could be used in later experiments. The 6-MP has not been found in healthy human serum. As shown in Table 2 , The average recoveries of spiked 6-MP in human serum ranged from 103.5% to 106.6% and RSD was from 1.7% to 3.5%. Thus, the proposed ratiometric probe offers a practical application for the rapid determination of 6-MP in human serum samples.

In conclusion, Zn-doped CdTe QDs (ZnCdTe QDs) was successfully synthesized by one-step aqueous route and was mixed with blue fluorescent CDs to fabricating ratiometric fluorescent probe for 6-MP determination. The yellow fluorescence of ZnCdTe QDs and blue fluorescence of CDs exhibited difference response to the 6-MP. The fluorescence of ZnCdTe QDs was selectively quenched based on the FRET while CDs did not showed obvious change in fluorescence signal. The prepared probe was successfully constructed for 6-MP determination and had a comparable detection limit, high sensitivity and competitive detection speed. Furthermore, the methods still utilized for detecting 6-MP in human serum. The ratiometric probes have biological significance for monitoring inappropriate concentrations of 6-MP in serum.

Tian Zhang: Writing – original draft, Conducting experiments, writing original draft preparation. Zhiwen Gan: Data curation. Shujun Zhen: Investigation. Yongmei Hu: Supervision. Xiaoli Hu: Writing – review & editing, Reviewing and editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21974109) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. XDJK2019TY003).
